focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The Bebop met them with red dust pasted into the seams of their clothes and slick on the ramp treads. The ship smelled like old grease, cold fryer oil tracked up from the market, and the bell peppers Jet had scorched earlier and never quite aired out. The hull ticked as it cooled. Somewhere below, water moved in the lines and a pump whined itself off again.

The silence followed them into the common room.

Faye had the chip in her fist so long the corners had stamped a square into her palm. The pilot had pressed it there in the market, quick as a dealer palming a card, then gone sideways through the crowd before Faye could decide whether to grab her or hit her or ask the one question that had actually mattered. She could still see the girl's face in flashes between bodies and hanging strips of plastic awning. Calm. Almost polite.

You're looking at this backwards.

She crossed the common room, dropped the chip on the bolted table, and let it spin once before it settled.

"There," she said. "That's what she gave me."

Jet shut the hatch behind them harder than he had to. "That's it?"

"No, she also gave me a detailed explanation of her motives and a nice basket." Faye peeled off her jacket and threw it at the couch. "Yeah, that's it."

Spike stayed near the porthole, one shoulder against the wall, looking like he had not decided whether he was in the room or just waiting until he could leave it. His hair still had market dust in it. There was a cigarette behind one ear, unlit. He had reached for it every ten minutes on the walk back and never lit it. Maybe he did not want Jet starting in. Maybe he did not want the smoke. He looked at the chip and then at Faye.

"What'd she say?" he asked.

Faye heard the line again, dry and level, not dramatic at all. That...
prev_tail_excerpt:
Faye stared at the cryogenic-facility node like she could burn a hole through it by refusing to blink. "Two years of this and she parks in our spot like she's picking a fight over lunch."

"Maybe because that's how you get people out of the house," Ed said.

"Not people," Spike said.

That was when he stood up straight.

It was such a small change, two inches maybe, the lazy angle gone out of his spine. But Spike lived in slouches and doorframes and sideways leans. Seeing him vertical was like hearing a glass crack in another room and realizing it was your glass.

Jet saw it too. Faye did. Even Ein lifted his head.

Spike crushed the cigarette out in a saucer. "Us."

The vent fan turned.

The refrigerator hummed, failed, and quieted.

Jet's metal fingers quit clicking. Faye stared at Spike like he'd just named the thing she'd been living inside. Even Ein stayed upright, ears tipped forward.

Jet looked at the Europa node a long time. When he spoke again, the words came out flat and practical, which meant they had cost him. "If she wants us following the trail, fine. Tomorrow we stop following it blind. We answer the first knock and make her come the rest of the way."

Faye's eyes cut to him. "With what?"

Jet didn't look away from the screen. "Whatever she went looking for on Europa, she can come look at it in person."

Nobody jumped on him. On the Bebop that was already halfway to a plan.

Faye looked him over once, slow and merciless. "You're gonna need a hat."

"No," Jet said immediately.

Spike, still standing straight, said, "Yeah."

Faye set her bowl down and headed for storage before Jet could come up with a second no.
